introduction: Profit and Loss measures how much money is gained or lost when an article is sold compared with its cost price. Competitive exams test direct percentage calculations, reverse problems (finding CP from SP), successive discounts, false weights, and marked-price scenarios. The key is always identifying CP, SP, profit or loss, and whether percentages are on CP or SP.
explanation:
CONCEPT EXPLANATION

Cost Price (CP) is what the seller pays to acquire an item. Selling Price (SP) is what the buyer pays. If SP > CP, there is Profit = SP − CP. If SP < CP, there is Loss = CP − SP.

Profit % is always calculated on CP unless stated otherwise: Profit % = (Profit / CP) × 100. Loss % = (Loss / CP) × 100.

When gain or loss percent is given, SP = CP × (100 ± gain or loss %) / 100. To find CP from SP after a known gain: CP = SP × 100 / (100 + gain %).

Marked Price (MP) is the tagged price before discount. Discount is usually on MP: SP = MP × (100 − discount %) / 100.

Successive discounts of a% and b% combine as a single equivalent discount: 100 − [(100 − a)(100 − b) / 100], not a + b.

REAL-LIFE APPLICATIONS

Shopkeepers set markup and discount during sales, businesses analyse margins on inventory, and investors compare returns on purchases versus resale value using the same profit-percent framework.

COMMON MISTAKES

1) Calculating profit % on SP instead of CP.
2) Adding successive discounts instead of multiplying remaining price fractions.
3) Forgetting that a 20% loss followed by 20% gain does not break even.
4) Confusing MP with CP when only discount is given.
5) Ignoring extra charges (transport, tax) that should be added to CP.

IMPORTANT NOTES

• Profit % and Loss % are on CP by convention in aptitude exams.
• Equal profit and loss percentages do not cancel; net result is always a loss.
• If CP of x articles equals SP of y articles, profit % = [(x − y) / y] × 100 when x > y.
• False weight problems: treat declared weight as SP basis and actual weight as CP basis.
• Always write CP and SP in the same currency unit before comparing.

formulas.title: Profit and Loss
formulas.content: Profit = SP − CP; Loss = CP − SP

formulas.title: SP from CP and gain or loss
formulas.content: SP = CP × (100 + gain %) / 100 or SP = CP × (100 − loss %) / 100

formulas.title: CP from SP and gain or loss
formulas.content: CP = SP × 100 / (100 + gain %) or CP = SP × 100 / (100 − loss %)

formulas.title: Discount and Marked Price
formulas.content: Discount = MP − SP; SP = MP × (100 − discount %) / 100

formulas.title: Successive discounts
formulas.content: Equivalent single discount after a% and b% = 100 − [(100 − a)(100 − b) / 100]

formulas.title: CP equals SP of fewer articles
formulas.content: If CP of x items = SP of y items (x > y), Profit % = [(x − y) / y] × 100

shortcutTips.title: CP anchor
shortcutTips.tip: Assume CP = ₹100 whenever only percentages are given; profit or loss amount equals the percentage directly.

shortcutTips.title: Reverse gain
shortcutTips.tip: SP at x% gain means CP = SP × 100 / (100 + x); at x% loss, CP = SP × 100 / (100 − x).

shortcutTips.title: Successive discounts
shortcutTips.tip: Multiply remaining fractions: 10% then 20% off means pay 0.9 × 0.8 = 0.72 of MP (28% total off).

shortcutTips.title: Equal % swing
shortcutTips.tip: Same % profit then loss on CP gives net loss of (x/10)² percent — e.g. 10% each way → 1% net loss.

shortcutTips.title: Article ratio trick
shortcutTips.tip: CP of 5 sold at SP of 4 → loss of 1 on 5, i.e. 20% loss on CP.

shortcutTips.title: False weight
shortcutTips.tip: Gain % from false weight = [(True weight − False weight) / False weight] × 100 when selling at declared weight price.

solvedExamples.problem: A shopkeeper buys an article for ₹450 and sells it for ₹540. Find the profit percentage.
By selling an article for ₹920, a man gains 15%. What is the cost price?
The marked price of a shirt is ₹800. After two successive discounts of 10% and 15%, find the selling price.
A trader sells rice using a weight 900 g instead of 1 kg but charges the price of 1 kg. Find his gain percent.
steps: CP corresponds to 900 g; SP corresponds to 1000 g.
